claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I have a child. My son Alex is 18. He's not in school or college at the moment though - he finished his education a while back.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 18 and no longer in approved education or training, he would generally fall outside the normal Child Benefit eligibility criteria, as Child Benefit typically continues only until age 16, or up to age 20 if the child is in approved education or training.

However, let me ask a clarifying question to make sure I have the full picture:

**Has Alex registered with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with a careers service or anything like that. He just finished school and has been at home since then.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Let me ask another question to fully understand your situation:

**Are you currently living in the UK, and is Alex living with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Birmingham. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have one more important question:

**Has Alex started any paid work, or is he receiving any benefits such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working and he's not receiving any benefits in his own name either. He's just been at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've provided, I'm unable to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:

According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify if you're responsible for a child **under 16**. For children aged 16 and over, you'll usually still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is **under 20 and they stay in approved education or training**.

Since Alex is 18 and is no longer in approved education or training, and he hasn't registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, he would not meet the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

However, I want to make sure I haven't missed anything. Let me ask:

**Is there any possibility that Alex might be registered or about to register with a careers service or the armed services? Or is there any other circumstance about Alex's situation that I should know about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, there's nothing else - he's not planning to register with a careers service or the armed forces. He's just at home at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. I now have all the information I need to provide you with a final assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary:**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

**Why:** Child Benefit typically covers children under 16. For 16-19 year olds, eligibility continues only if they remain in approved education or training. Since Alex is 18 and has finished school without registering with a careers service or the armed services, he no longer qualifies.

If Alex were to register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks. I'd recommend contacting the Child Benefit Office if Alex's circumstances change in the future.

Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and no longer in approved education or training. According to Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16. For children aged 16 or over, eligibility continues only if they are under 20 and staying in approved education or training, or if they have registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services within 8 weeks of leaving education. Since Alex has finished school, is not in education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services, he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Although you live in the UK and Alex lives with you, he is 18 years old and does not meet the age and education requirements for Child Benefit eligibility. Child Benefit for 16-19 year olds is only available if they remain in approved education or training, or register with a careers service or armed forces shortly after leaving education - none of which applies to Alex's current situation.">
